 {"id":"wa-29k1","title":"SnapshotEngine orchestrator — coordinate full mux state capture","description":"## Goal
Implement the central SnapshotEngine that orchestrates the full mux state capture process — coordinating layout capture, scrollback capture, process resolution, and agent session binding into a unified MuxSnapshot, then persisting it to SQLite.

## Background & Motivation
This is the "brain" of the snapshot system. It ties together all the individual capture components (schema from bd-cuz, storage from bd-nz6, scrollback from bd-ybq, layout from bd-2t2) into a single coherent workflow. Without this orchestrator, the individual components are useful but can't produce a complete, consistent snapshot.

The design draws inspiration from WezTerm's resurrect.wezterm plugin (which does periodic_save() in Lua) but implements it natively in Rust with wa's async runtime for much better performance and reliability. Unlike the Lua plugin which can only capture what the Lua API exposes, wa's SnapshotEngine has direct access to the mux protocol via the vendored DirectMuxClient.

## Technical Design

### Struct Definition
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/engine.rs (new file)
pub struct SnapshotEngine {
    storage: Arc<StorageManager>,
    mux_client: Arc<DirectMuxClient>,  // or pool when bd-41w lands
    config: SnapshotConfig,
    last_snapshot_hash: RwLock<Option<String>>,
    in_progress: AtomicBool,
}

pub struct SnapshotConfig {
    pub enabled: bool,
    pub interval_seconds: u64,        // default: 300 (5 min)
    pub max_scrollback_lines: usize,  // default: 10000
    pub max_concurrent_captures: usize, // default: 10
    pub retention_count: usize,       // default: 10
    pub retention_days: u64,          // default: 7
}

pub enum SnapshotTrigger {
    Periodic,
    Manual,
    PreRestart,
    PreShutdown,
}
```

### Core Method: capture_snapshot()
```rust
pub async fn capture_snapshot(&self, trigger: SnapshotTrigger) -> Result<MuxSnapshot> {
    // 1. Check if another snapshot is in progress
    if self.in_progress.swap(true, Ordering::SeqCst) {
        return Err(Error::SnapshotInProgress);
    }
    let _guard = scopeguard::guard((), |_| self.in_progress.store(false, Ordering::Release));

    // 2. Capture layout tree (from bd-2t2)
    let layout = self.capture_layout().await?;
    
    // 3. Compute pane list hash — skip if unchanged since last snapshot
    let pane_hash = compute_pane_hash(&layout);
    if trigger == SnapshotTrigger::Periodic {
        if let Some(ref last) = *self.last_snapshot_hash.read().await {
            if *last == pane_hash {
                return Err(Error::NoChanges);
            }
        }
    }
    
    // 4. Capture scrollback for all panes in parallel (from bd-ybq)
    let scrollbacks = self.capture_scrollbacks_parallel(&layout).await?;
    
    // 5. Resolve agent sessions from wa's AgentSessionRecord table
    let agent_refs = self.resolve_agent_sessions(&layout).await?;
    
    // 6. Assemble MuxSnapshot (schema from bd-cuz)
    let snapshot = MuxSnapshot {
        version: 1,
        timestamp: Utc::now(),
        trigger,
        layout,
        scrollbacks,
        agent_refs,
        checksum: String::new(), // computed below
    };
    
    // 7. Compute and set checksum
    let checksum = compute_snapshot_checksum(&snapshot);
    
    // 8. Persist to SQLite (storage from bd-nz6)
    let snapshot_id = self.storage.save_snapshot(&snapshot).await?;
    
    // 9. Update last hash
    *self.last_snapshot_hash.write().await = Some(pane_hash);
    
    // 10. Run retention cleanup
    self.storage.cleanup_old_snapshots(&self.config).await?;
    
    Ok(snapshot)
}
```

### Periodic Snapshot Loop
Integrates with wa's existing tokio runtime:
```rust
pub async fn run_periodic(&self, mut shutdown: tokio::sync::watch::Receiver<bool>) {
    let mut interval = tokio::time::interval(Duration::from_secs(self.config.interval_seconds));
    loop {
        tokio::select! {
            _ = interval.tick() => {
                match self.capture_snapshot(SnapshotTrigger::Periodic).await {
                    Ok(snap) => info!("Periodic snapshot saved: {}", snap.id),
                    Err(Error::NoChanges) => debug!("No changes, skipping snapshot"),
                    Err(Error::SnapshotInProgress) => debug!("Snapshot already in progress"),
                    Err(e) => warn!("Periodic snapshot failed: {}", e),
                }
            }
            _ = shutdown.changed() => break,
        }
    }
}
```

### Configuration (wa.toml)
```toml
[snapshots]
enabled = true
interval_seconds = 300
max_scrollback_lines = 10000
max_concurrent_captures = 10
retention_count = 10
retention_days = 7
```

## Integration Points
- **StorageManager** (crates/wa-core/src/storage.rs): Add snapshot-related methods
- **DirectMuxClient** (crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs): Use for pane listing and scrollback
- **Config** (crates/wa-core/src/config.rs): Add SnapshotConfig section
- **Event bus**: Emit SnapshotCompleted events for wa's workflow engine
- **Watch daemon** (wa watch): Start periodic snapshot loop alongside capture loop

## Key Files to Create/Modify
- CREATE: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/engine.rs
- CREATE: crates/wa-core/src/snapshot/mod.rs
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/config.rs (add SnapshotConfig)
- MODIFY: crates/wa-core/src/storage.rs (add snapshot methods)
- MODIFY: crates/wa/src/main.rs (wire into watch daemon)

## Dependencies
- bd-cuz: MuxSnapshot schema (defines the data structures)
- bd-nz6: SQLite tables (provides persistence layer)
- bd-ybq: Scrollback capture (provides per-pane content extraction)
- bd-2t2: Layout tree capture (provides window/tab/pane hierarchy)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Periodic snapshots run at configured interval
- On-demand snapshots complete and return snapshot_id
- No-op detection works (skips when nothing changed since last snapshot)
- Concurrent scrollback capture respects max_concurrent_captures limit
- Agent session references are correctly populated from wa's session table
- Events emitted on snapshot completion for wa's workflow engine
- Retention cleanup runs after each snapshot (both count and age limits)
- Pre-restart snapshots block until complete (no timeout)
- Graceful degradation: partial failures (e.g., one pane's scrollback fails) don't abort the entire snapshot

## Estimated Effort
3-4 hours implementation, 1-2 hours testing

## macOS Compatibility
On macOS, process state inspection differs from Linux. Where the design references `/proc/<pid>/status` for resolving process state during snapshot capture, use `proc_pidinfo()` from `libproc` instead:
```rust
#[cfg(target_os = "macos")]
fn get_process_state(pid: i32) -> Result<ProcessState> {
    use libproc::libproc::proc_pid;
    let info = proc_pid::pidinfo::<proc_pid::BSDInfo>(pid, 0)?;
    // Map BSD process flags to our ProcessState enum
    Ok(ProcessState::from_bsd_flags(info.pbi_flags))
}

#[cfg(target_os = "linux")]
fn get_process_state(pid: i32) -> Result<ProcessState> {
    let status = std::fs::read_to_string(format!("/proc/{}/status", pid))?;
    // Parse "State:" line
    Ok(ProcessState::from_proc_status(&status))
}
```
Add `libproc` to Cargo.toml dependencies (macOS-only): `libproc = { version = "0.14", optional = true }`.

## Test Requirements

### Concurrency Testing (LabRuntime DPOR)
Use LabRuntime with Dynamic Partial Order Reduction to verify concurrent snapshot coordination:
- Verify that two concurrent `capture_snapshot()` calls never both proceed (the `in_progress` AtomicBool guard works correctly under all interleavings)
- Verify that periodic snapshot + manual snapshot don't corrupt shared state (`last_snapshot_hash` RwLock)
- Verify that concurrent scrollback captures (the parallel `capture_scrollbacks_parallel`) don't race on the DirectMuxClient connection pool
- Verify that snapshot + retention cleanup don't delete a snapshot that's still being written

### Criterion Benchmarks
Add benchmarks in `crates/wa-core/benches/snapshot_engine.rs`:
- `bench_snapshot_creation_200_panes`: Full snapshot of 200-pane mux state must complete in <100ms (excluding I/O)
- `bench_snapshot_no_change_detection`: Hash comparison for no-op detection, target <1ms
- `bench_snapshot_checksum`: Checksum computation for a 200-pane snapshot
- `bench_retention_cleanup`: Cleanup of 100 old snapshots from SQLite

### Property-Based Testing (proptest)
Add proptest cases in `crates/wa-core/tests/proptest_snapshot_engine.rs`:
- **State machine transitions**: Generate arbitrary sequences of `(SnapshotTrigger, MuxStateChange)` events and verify the engine never enters an inconsistent state (e.g., `in_progress` stuck true, `last_snapshot_hash` out of sync with actual last snapshot)
- **Config validation**: `arb_snapshot_config()` generates random configs; verify the engine accepts all valid configs and rejects invalid ones (zero interval, zero retention, etc.)
- **Partial failure resilience**: Generate random failure injection points during `capture_snapshot()`; verify the engine always resets `in_progress` and never leaves partial snapshots in storage

## Cross-References
- **wa-3kxe.3** (Differential snapshots): The `last_snapshot_hash` and no-op detection in this engine are the foundation for differential snapshots. wa-3kxe.3 extends this with fine-grained per-pane change tracking instead of the coarse whole-layout hash used here.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:31:26.999046Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T21:01:31.021039-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T21:01:31.021039-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented: snapshot_engine.rs with SnapshotEngine orchestrator, 12 passing tests. Captures topology + pane state + dedup hash, persists to SQLite, periodic loop with retention cleanup.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-29k1","depends_on_id":"wa-rsaf","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:34:57.878324Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-29k1","depends_on_id":"bd-cuz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:14.244907Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-29k1","depends_on_id":"bd-nz6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:14.341449Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-29k1","depends_on_id":"bd-ybq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:14.424255Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-29k1","depends_on_id":"bd-2t2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:14.503448Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2abzy","title":"Replace tokio channels (mpsc/watch/broadcast) with asupersync","description":"# Replace tokio channels with asupersync

## Background
Channels are the most widespread async primitive in FrankenTerm. tokio channels are used for event passing, cancellation, state sync, and IPC.

## Scope
- tokio::sync::mpsc → asupersync::channel::mpsc (two-phase reserve/commit)
- tokio::sync::watch → asupersync::channel::watch
- tokio::sync::broadcast → asupersync::channel::broadcast

## Critical change: Two-phase send
asupersync channels use reserve/commit pattern:
```rust
// Before (tokio)
tx.send(msg).await?;  // Can lose msg if cancelled between enqueue and ack

// After (asupersync)
let permit = tx.reserve(cx).await?;  // Phase 1: reserve slot (cancellation safe)
permit.send(msg);                     // Phase 2: commit (infallible after reservation)
```

This eliminates the "message lost on cancellation" class of bugs but requires updating ALL channel send sites.

## Affected modules (high-impact)
- runtime.rs — 38+ mpsc uses for observation/command loops
- ipc.rs — 22+ uses for IPC message passing
- tailer.rs — capture event channels
- mcp.rs — MCP tool response channels
- mux_client.rs — pane output subscription channels
- native_events.rs — event listener channels

## Watch channel migration
Watch channels are used for cancellation signaling:
```rust
// Before
let (cancel_tx, cancel_rx) = tokio::sync::watch::channel(false);
// Signal cancellation
cancel_tx.send(true)?;
// Check cancellation
if *cancel_rx.borrow() { break; }

// After — consider using cx.checkpoint()? instead for cancellation
// or asupersync::channel::watch with Cx-aware API
```

## Acceptance criteria
- All tokio channel types replaced with asupersync equivalents
- Two-phase send pattern adopted at all send sites
- Watch-based cancellation migrated to cx.checkpoint() where appropriate
- No message loss under cancellation (the whole point!)
- Existing channel tests pass

## Concurrency verification
- **Loom model checking**: Use Loom to verify correctness of all replaced channel types:
  - MPSC: verify no message loss, no duplication, FIFO ordering under all interleavings
  - Watch: verify last-value semantics, no torn reads
  - Broadcast: verify all subscribers receive all messages, no message loss on slow consumers
  - Two-phase reserve/commit: verify that reserved permits are never leaked and that commit is truly infallible after successful reserve
  - Add `tests/loom_channels.rs` with separate `loom::model` blocks for each channel type.

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for channel throughput**: Add `benches/channel_throughput.rs` measuring:
  - MPSC single-producer single-consumer throughput (messages/sec)
  - MPSC multi-producer single-consumer throughput (2, 4, 8 producers)
  - Watch update + read throughput
  - Broadcast fan-out throughput (2, 4, 8 subscribers)
  - Two-phase reserve/commit overhead vs tokio's direct send
  - Include tokio baseline groups for direct comparison.

## Property-based testing
- **Proptest for message ordering**: Use proptest to generate arbitrary sequences of sends across multiple producers and verify that per-producer FIFO ordering is preserved at the consumer. Generate random interleaving schedules and verify no reordering, duplication, or loss occurs.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:48:24.270254Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:47:40.45912Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2abzy","depends_on_id":"wa-3d14m","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:57.585586Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
-{"id":"wa-2ahu0","title":"Memory pressure engine — per-pane budgets, eviction, and compression","description":"# Memory Pressure Engine

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization, /alien-artifact-coding

## Problem
200 agents × 500MB = 100GB baseline. Long-lived agents grow to 1-10GB+ each. Without pressure management, OOM killer strikes randomly, potentially killing the mux server itself.

## Solution

### 1. Per-pane memory tracking
- **Linux**: Sample /proc/[pid]/statm every 30s for each pane process tree
- **macOS**: Use proc_pidinfo(PROC_PIDTASKINFO) for per-process RSS, virtual, shared memory
- Track RSS, virtual, shared memory per pane
- Compute total memory pressure: sum(pane RSS) / available RAM

### 2. Memory pressure tiers
- **Linux**: Keyed to /proc/pressure/memory PSI metrics
- **macOS**: Keyed to vm_statistics64 (free + inactive pages ratio) and os_proc_available_memory()
- avg10 < 10% (or macOS equivalent): Green — no action
- avg10 10-20%: Yellow — compress idle pane scrollback, warn user
- avg10 20-40%: Orange — evict oldest idle pane scrollback to disk, pause captures
- avg10 > 40%: Red — kill largest idle pane, emergency scrollback eviction

### 3. Scrollback compression and eviction
- Compress scrollback for panes idle > 5 min (zstd, ~10:1 ratio)
- Evict scrollback to disk for panes idle > 30 min
- Restore transparently on pane focus

### 4. OOM score adjustment
- **Linux**: Set oom_score_adj to protect mux server (-1000)
- **macOS**: No OOM score equivalent; use Jetsam priority via memorystatus_control (if available) or advisory
- Set agent panes proportional to age: newer = lower score, older = higher score
- Ensures OOM killer targets oldest idle agents first

### 5. Formal memory bound (/alien-artifact-coding)
Prove that the mux server own memory usage is bounded by O(n) where n = pane count, independent of scrollback size. Use formal methods.

## Tests
- Simulate memory pressure ramp, verify tier transitions
- Measure scrollback compression ratio and latency
- Verify OOM score adj protects mux server (Linux)
- 200-pane memory stress test: no OOM with 128GB limit
- **Use criterion benchmarks for compression throughput**
- **Test both Linux and macOS code paths**

## Acceptance criteria
- Memory pressure monitored via PSI (Linux) / vm_statistics64 (macOS)
- Scrollback compression saves >80% memory for idle panes
- Disk eviction transparent to user
- Mux server never killed by OOM
- Memory bound formally verified","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:11:54.455402Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T17:04:43.72591Z"}

## Problem
200 agents × 500MB = 100GB baseline. Long-lived agents grow to 1-10GB+ each. Without pressure management, OOM killer strikes randomly, potentially killing the mux server itself.

## Solution

### 1. Per-pane memory tracking
- **Linux**: Sample /proc/[pid]/statm every 30s for each pane process tree
- **macOS**: Use proc_pidinfo(PROC_PIDTASKINFO) for per-process RSS, virtual, shared memory
- Track RSS, virtual, shared memory per pane
- Compute total memory pressure: sum(pane RSS) / available RAM

### 2. Memory pressure tiers
- **Linux**: Keyed to /proc/pressure/memory PSI metrics
- **macOS**: Keyed to vm_statistics64 (free + inactive pages ratio) and os_proc_available_memory()
- avg10 < 10% (or macOS equivalent): Green — no action
- avg10 10-20%: Yellow — compress idle pane scrollback, warn user
- avg10 20-40%: Orange — evict oldest idle pane scrollback to disk, pause captures
- avg10 > 40%: Red — kill largest idle pane, emergency scrollback eviction

- **macOS**: No OOM score equivalent; use Jetsam priority via memorystatus_control (if available) or advisory

 {"id":"wa-2apg5","title":"Zellij session management analysis — persistence, reconnect, resurrection","description":"# Zellij Session Management Analysis

## Why this matters
Zellij has built-in session persistence and resurrection — a user can detach and reattach, and sessions survive server restarts. FrankenTerm's session persistence epic (wa-rsaf) is building this from scratch. Understanding how Zellij solved it could save significant design effort.

## What to analyze

### Session lifecycle
- How are sessions created, listed, attached, detached, destroyed?
- What state is persisted and where (filesystem, database, memory)?
- What happens on unexpected server crash vs clean shutdown?

### Session resurrection
- Can Zellij restore sessions after a server restart?
- What state is lost vs preserved (scrollback? running processes? environment?)
- How does it handle process re-launch (or does it?)

### Multi-client attachment
- Can multiple clients attach to the same session simultaneously?
- How are conflicts handled (input routing, resize, permissions)?
- This is directly relevant to FrankenTerm's agent swarm use case

### Session serialization format
- What format does Zellij use to serialize session state?
- How does it handle layout topology (splits, tabs, floating panes)?
- Compare to our planned MuxSnapshot schema (bd-cuz)

## FrankenTerm implications
For each finding, document: "FrankenTerm could use this by..." with a concrete implementation sketch.

## Acceptance criteria
- Session lifecycle fully documented
- Resurrection mechanism analyzed (what survives, what doesn't)
- Multi-client behavior analyzed
- Serialization format documented
- 3+ actionable recommendations for FrankenTerm

## Cross-references
- **wa-rsaf** (session persistence epic): Findings from this analysis directly inform the design of FrankenTerm's session persistence system. Specific areas of impact include: session serialization format (compare with MuxSnapshot schema bd-cuz), resurrection strategy (what state to persist and how), and multi-client attachment semantics for agent swarms.

## Notes
- This is one of the highest-value Zellij analysis beads because FrankenTerm's session persistence (wa-rsaf) is being designed from scratch. Zellij's battle-tested approach to session resurrection can save significant design iteration.
- Pay special attention to what state Zellij does NOT persist — these are likely the hard problems where persistence has diminishing returns.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:53:42.044778Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:42:29.320403Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2apg5","depends_on_id":"wa-okyhm","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:54:56.544663Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2apg5","depends_on_id":"wa-22o1q","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T15:55:01.973841Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

 {"id":"wa-2bai5","title":"Zellij analysis synthesis — improvement roadmap for FrankenTerm mux server","description":"# Zellij Analysis Synthesis

## Goal
Synthesize ALL Zellij analysis findings into a prioritized, actionable improvement roadmap for FrankenTerm mux server.

## Deliverable: Comparison report with three sections

### 1. Quick wins (low effort, high value)
Ideas we can adopt immediately with minimal refactoring. Each with:
- What Zellij does
- How FrankenTerm would implement it
- Estimated effort (hours/days)
- Which existing beads it relates to

### 2. Strategic improvements (medium effort, high value)
Architectural ideas worth investing in. Each with:
- Design sketch
- Migration path from current architecture
- Risk assessment
- New beads to create

### 3. Future considerations (high effort or uncertain value)
Ideas worth tracking but not immediately actionable. Each with:
- Why it interesting
- What would need to change in FrankenTerm
- Conditions under which it becomes worth doing

## Cross-reference (MUST link findings to these epics)
- wa-rsaf (session persistence) — session management findings
- wa-3kxe (fork hardening) — architecture hardening findings
- wa-3cyp (performance optimization) — throughput/scaling findings
- wa-e34d9 (asupersync migration) — async runtime findings
- wa-iehgn (ultra-performance) — resource management, scheduling findings
- wa-dr6zv (/dp tool integration) — plugin/extensibility findings
- bd-9dp (tiered update rates) — output throttling findings
- wa-3r5e (scrollback memory) — memory management findings
- **wa-3bja.5** (Ghostty analysis synthesis): Both synthesis reports should be compared side-by-side to produce a unified improvement roadmap. Where Zellij and Ghostty solve the same problem differently, document both approaches and recommend the best fit for FrankenTerm.

## Unified Roadmap Note
After both wa-2bai5 (this bead) and wa-3bja.5 (Ghostty synthesis) are complete, a comparison pass should identify:
- Patterns where both Zellij and Ghostty converge (high-confidence recommendations)
- Patterns where they diverge (requires FrankenTerm-specific analysis)
- Unique innovations from each that have no counterpart in the other

## Acceptance criteria
- All 6 analysis beads synthesized
- Prioritized roadmap with 5+ actionable recommendations
- Each recommendation has implementation sketch and effort estimate
- Cross-references to ALL relevant existing beads (not just wa-rsaf/wa-3kxe/wa-3cyp/wa-e34d9)
- New beads created for any recommendations that do not map to existing work

 {"id":"wa-2c50","title":"Protocol error auto-recovery — reconnect on UnexpectedResponse","description":"## Goal
Implement automatic recovery from WezTerm mux protocol errors (UnexpectedResponse, codec failures) by detecting corrupted connection state, poisoning the connection, and transparently reconnecting. Build on the existing circuit_breaker.rs and retry.rs modules.

## Background & Motivation
Under heavy load, the WezTerm mux protocol can enter a corrupted state where the client and server are "out of sync" on the PDU stream. This manifests as UnexpectedResponse errors (e.g., getting UnitResponse when expecting ListPanesResponse). The error is defined at mux_client.rs line 83.

Currently, wa's RetryWith mechanism retries with the SAME connection, which has corrupted state. This makes the retry fail too, creating a cascade of errors. The fix is to detect protocol corruption, discard the bad connection, and create a fresh one.

## Existing Code (BUILD ON, don't reinvent)
- **circuit_breaker.rs**: CircuitBreaker with Open/HalfOpen/Closed states, configurable failure threshold and recovery timeout. Already handles "stop trying after too many failures" pattern.
- **retry.rs**: RetryPolicy with configurable max_retries, backoff strategy (constant, linear, exponential), jitter. Already handles "try again with backoff" pattern.
- **pool.rs**: Pool<C> with health_check(), idle eviction, semaphore concurrency limiting.
- **degradation.rs**: DegradationManager with Subsystem::WeztermCli for reporting mux failures.

## Technical Design

### Error Classification
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs

pub enum ProtocolErrorKind {
    Recoverable,   // UnexpectedResponse, timeout — reconnect and retry
    Permanent,     // Version mismatch, auth failure — give up
    Transient,     // Server busy, try again later
}

impl DirectMuxClient {
    fn classify_error(&self, err: &Error) -> ProtocolErrorKind {
        match err {
            Error::UnexpectedResponse { .. } => ProtocolErrorKind::Recoverable,
            Error::IoError(e) if e.kind() == ErrorKind::BrokenPipe => ProtocolErrorKind::Recoverable,
            Error::IoError(e) if e.kind() == ErrorKind::ConnectionReset => ProtocolErrorKind::Recoverable,
            Error::CodecVersionMismatch => ProtocolErrorKind::Permanent,
            _ => ProtocolErrorKind::Transient,
        }
    }
}
```

### Auto-Recovery with Circuit Breaker Integration
```rust
// Wire error classification into existing CircuitBreaker + RetryPolicy:
pub struct ResilientMuxPool {
    pool: DirectMuxClientPool,
    circuit_breaker: CircuitBreaker,  // from circuit_breaker.rs
    retry_policy: RetryPolicy,        // from retry.rs
    degradation: Arc<DegradationManager>,
}

impl ResilientMuxPool {
    pub async fn execute<F, T>(&self, op: F) -> Result<T>
    where
        F: Fn(&DirectMuxClient) -> Pin<Box<dyn Future<Output = Result<T>>>>,
    {
        // Check circuit breaker first
        if !self.circuit_breaker.allow_request() {
            return Err(Error::CircuitOpen);
        }

        let result = self.retry_policy.execute(|| async {
            let conn = self.pool.get().await?;
            match op(&conn).await {
                Ok(result) => {
                    self.circuit_breaker.record_success();
                    Ok(result)
                }
                Err(e) => {
                    let kind = conn.classify_error(&e);
                    match kind {
                        ProtocolErrorKind::Recoverable => {
                            conn.mark_poisoned(); // pool will create fresh
                            self.circuit_breaker.record_failure();
                            Err(e) // retry with fresh connection
                        }
                        ProtocolErrorKind::Permanent => {
                            self.circuit_breaker.record_failure();
                            self.degradation.report_failure(Subsystem::WeztermCli, &e.to_string()).await;
                            Err(Error::Permanent(e)) // don't retry
                        }
                        ProtocolErrorKind::Transient => {
                            Err(e) // retry after backoff
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }).await;

        result
    }
}
```

### Frame Resynchronization
For cases where the PDU stream is corrupted mid-frame:
```rust
/// If read_next_pdu() fails to decode, skip bytes until a valid PDU header is found
async fn resync_stream(&mut self) -> Result<()> {
    let mut buf = [0u8; 1];
    let mut skipped = 0;
    loop {
        self.stream.read_exact(&mut buf).await?;
        skipped += 1;
        if self.try_parse_pdu_header().is_ok() {
            warn!("Resynchronized PDU stream after skipping {} bytes", skipped);
            return Ok(());
        }
        if skipped > 1_000_000 {
            return Err(Error::StreamCorrupted);
        }
    }
}
```

## Existing Code References
- **UnexpectedResponse**: crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs line 83
- **CircuitBreaker**: crates/wa-core/src/circuit_breaker.rs — Open/HalfOpen/Closed FSM
- **RetryPolicy**: crates/wa-core/src/retry.rs — exponential backoff with jitter
- **Pool<C>**: crates/wa-core/src/pool.rs — has mark_poisoned() / is_healthy() pattern
- **DegradationManager**: crates/wa-core/src/degradation.rs — Subsystem::WeztermCli

## Expected Impact
- Protocol errors no longer cascade into sustained failures
- Transparent to callers — just a brief retry delay
- Circuit breaker prevents hammering an unhealthy mux server
- Combined with connection pool, makes wa resilient to mux server instability

## Dependencies
- bd-41w (Connection pool): Required for transparent reconnection

## Acceptance Criteria
- UnexpectedResponse triggers connection poisoning and retry
- Retry with fresh connection succeeds
- Frame resynchronization recovers from partial PDU reads
- Permanent errors (version mismatch) are NOT retried
- Circuit breaker activates after configurable failure threshold
- Integration with existing CircuitBreaker and RetryPolicy modules
- Metrics: recovery count, resync count, permanent failures, circuit breaker state

## Estimated Effort
3-4 hours implementation, 1 hour testing

## Concurrency Testing (LabRuntime DPOR)
- **LabRuntime with DPOR exploration** for concurrent reconnection scenarios:
  - Multiple tasks simultaneously discover a poisoned connection and attempt reconnection. DPOR explores all interleavings to verify that exactly one new connection is established (no thundering herd), the circuit breaker state transitions are linearizable, and no task observes a half-initialized connection.
  - Concurrent error classification: multiple tasks classify errors on the same connection simultaneously. DPOR verifies that classification is deterministic regardless of interleaving and that mark_poisoned() is idempotent under concurrent calls.
  - Circuit breaker state machine under concurrent load: DPOR explores interleavings where record_failure() and record_success() race, verifying the FSM never enters an invalid state (e.g., Open with zero failures).

## Benchmark Requirements
- **criterion benchmarks** with target `benches/protocol_recovery.rs`:
  - `reconnect_latency`: measure time from error detection to successful retry on a fresh connection. Target: <10ms for local Unix socket reconnection.
  - `error_classification_throughput`: measure classify_error() throughput. Target: >1M classifications/sec (it should be a simple match).
  - `circuit_breaker_state_transition`: measure record_failure/record_success cycle latency. Target: <100ns per state update.

## Property-Based Testing (proptest)
- **Error classification correctness**: for any Error variant, classify_error() returns a deterministic ProtocolErrorKind. Specifically: all UnexpectedResponse errors map to Recoverable, all CodecVersionMismatch errors map to Permanent, BrokenPipe and ConnectionReset map to Recoverable.
- **Circuit breaker consistency**: for any sequence of record_failure() and record_success() calls, the circuit breaker state is always valid (Closed with failures < threshold, Open after threshold crossed, HalfOpen after recovery timeout).
- **Retry idempotency**: retrying a Recoverable error with a fresh connection produces the same result as a first attempt on a healthy connection (no state leakage between attempts).

## Cross-References
- **wa-1l2o** (Error cascade circuit breaker): provides the broader error cascade prevention framework that this bead's per-connection circuit breaker integrates with; wa-1l2o manages cross-subsystem cascades while wa-2c50 handles mux-protocol-specific recovery.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:35:50.483477Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:42:53.435747Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2c50","depends_on_id":"bd-41w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:20.765571Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-2c50","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:36.165421Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2cha","title":"Mux server watchdog — health monitoring and auto-restart","description":"# Mux Server Watchdog — Health Monitoring and Auto-Restart

## Skills: /extreme-software-optimization

## Goal
Extend the existing watchdog module (watchdog.rs, 489 lines) with a MuxWatchdog component that monitors WezTerm mux server health (responsiveness, memory usage, connection acceptance) and can trigger alerts, degradation, or controlled restart via the safe-restart workflow (wa-1igc).

## Background
The mux server can become unresponsive without actually crashing — alive (process exists, port open) but not serving requests (lock contention, memory pressure, stuck event loop). Observed on css: 76GB RSS after 23 days, connections timing out.

## Existing Code (EXTEND, do not create from scratch)
- **watchdog.rs** (489 lines): HeartbeatRegistry with per-subsystem atomic timestamps, health_check() with configurable stall thresholds
- **degradation.rs** (989 lines): DegradationManager with Subsystem::WeztermCli already exists
- **backpressure.rs** (657 lines): BackpressureManager with 4-tier FSM

## MuxWatchdog Health Check
Every 30s:
1. **Ping**: list panes with timeout (5s default)
2. **Memory check**:
   - **Linux**: Read /proc/<pid>/status VmRSS field
   - **macOS**: Use proc_pidinfo(PROC_PIDTASKINFO) for resident_size, or `ps -o rss= -p <pid>`
3. Track health history in ring buffer (last 1000 samples)

## Escalation Logic
- 3 consecutive failures → DegradationManager alert (Subsystem::WeztermCli)
- 10 consecutive failures + auto_restart enabled → delegate to safe-restart workflow (wa-1igc)
- Memory > 32GB → warning
- Memory > 64GB → critical, report to DegradationManager

## Integration Points
- HeartbeatRegistry (watchdog.rs): Add mux_health atomic timestamp
- DegradationManager (degradation.rs): Use existing Subsystem::WeztermCli
- BackpressureMonitor: Feed health status into backpressure decisions
- Safe-restart (wa-1igc): Auto-restart delegates to snapshot→stop→start→restore

## Tests
- Health check detects unresponsive mux within 3 cycles
- Memory threshold alerts logged correctly
- **Criterion benchmarks**: health check overhead (<1ms per check)
- Test escalation: consecutive failures → degradation → restart trigger
- Test graceful handling of mux server not running at all
- **Test both Linux and macOS memory reading paths**

## Acceptance criteria
- Health checks run at configured interval
- Unresponsive mux detected within 3 check cycles
- Memory alerts reported to DegradationManager
- Auto-restart delegates to safe-restart workflow
- Works on Linux (/proc) and macOS (proc_pidinfo/ps)
- Health history available via wa robot API","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:36:17.627014Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T20:53:06.533797-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-10T20:53:06.533797-05:00","close_reason":"Mux server watchdog complete: MuxWatchdog in watchdog.rs with ping/memory checks, mux_watchdog.rs standalone module, degradation integration, wired into watcher","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2cha","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:39.678193Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2dd4s","title":"[EPIC] FrankenMux's Monster — Hybrid muxer combining WezTerm + Zellij innovations","description":"# FrankenMux's Monster

## Philosophy
Just as FrankenTerm takes the best pieces from different terminal projects, FrankenMux takes the best muxer ideas from WezTerm and Zellij and combines them into something superior to either.

WezTerm's mux has a robust wire protocol (PDU-based codec) and stable client-server architecture, but leaks memory and has suboptimal pane management. Zellij's mux has innovative layout management (floating panes, stacked layouts, swap layouts) and better resize handling, but uses a different architecture. FrankenMux combines WezTerm's protocol foundation with Zellij's layout innovations.

## What WezTerm mux does well
- Codec-based PDU wire protocol (efficient, versioned)
- Client-server separation (mux server is independent process)
- SSH domain support (mux over SSH tunnels)
- Tab/pane tree model (nested splits)

## What Zellij mux does better
- Floating panes (overlay without splits)
- Swap layouts (one-key layout switching)
- Stacked panes (vertical tabs within splits)
- Better resize handling (constraint-based, not proportional)
- Plugin-based pane content (WASI plugins)
- Session management (detach/attach with named sessions)

## FrankenMux target features
1. WezTerm wire protocol + PDU codec (keep)
2. Zellij-inspired floating pane overlay system (port)
3. Zellij-inspired swap layout system (port from Zig concepts, adapt)
4. Improved resize handling with constraints (hybrid)
5. Per-pane memory budgets with arena allocators (new, from wa-3axa)
6. Zero-copy scrollback sharing between panes (new)
7. Native event hooks replacing Lua callbacks (new, from wa-3dfxb)
8. asupersync-based async I/O (new, from wa-e34d9)

## Alien Artifact Quality Target
Apply /alien-artifact-coding methodology: formal correctness proofs for layout constraint solver, mathematical models for memory budgets, information-theoretic optimal PDU compression. Apply /extreme-software-optimization: profile-driven hot path optimization, zero-allocation steady-state, cache-line-aware data structures.

## Depends on
- wa-2umk2: FrankenTerm in-tree (own the mux source)
- wa-3dfxb: Lua elimination (clean mux from Lua deps)
- wa-e34d9: asupersync migration (unified async runtime)
- wa-3bja: Ghostty analysis (additional innovations to steal)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:53:17.880601Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T07:37:30.696541Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s","depends_on_id":"wa-2umk2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:37:30.364831Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s","depends_on_id":"wa-3dfxb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:37:30.471758Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s","depends_on_id":"wa-e34d9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:37:30.582175Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s","depends_on_id":"wa-3bja","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:37:30.696482Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
-{"id":"wa-2dd4s.1","title":"Analyze Zellij muxer architecture — layouts, floating panes, resize","description":"# Analyze Zellij Muxer Architecture

## Why
Zellij's muxer has innovations FrankenTerm wants to steal: floating panes, swap layouts, stacked panes, constraint-based resize. Before porting, we need a thorough architectural analysis.

## What to analyze

### Zellij layout system
- How layouts are defined (KDL format)
- How layouts are resolved into concrete pane positions
- Constraint solver: how pane sizes are computed during resize
- Swap layouts: how one-key layout switching works
- Floating panes: overlay system, z-ordering, focus management

### Zellij pane model
- Pane types: terminal, plugin, floating, stacked
- How pane state is managed (vs WezTerm's Pane trait + PaneId)
- How pane content is rendered (screen model differences)
- Plugin pane architecture (WASI plugins → relates to our WASM engine!)

### Zellij session management
- Named sessions: create, attach, detach
- Session resurrection: how state is persisted
- Multi-user sessions: shared session support

### Zellij resize handling
- Constraint-based vs WezTerm's proportional
- Minimum pane sizes
- Resize edge detection
- How resize propagates through the pane tree

## Deliverable
A markdown analysis document: `docs/zellij-analysis.md` containing:
1. Architecture diagrams (ASCII art)
2. Key data structures and their roles
3. Specific code locations for each innovation
4. Assessment: what to port vs what to reimagine
5. Dependency mapping: what Zellij concepts depend on others

## Repository
Zellij repo: https://github.com/zellij-org/zellij
Focus on: zellij-server/src/panes/, zellij-server/src/tab/, zellij-utils/src/layout.rs

## Testing
N/A — this is a research bead.  {"id":"wa-2dd4s.2","title":"Port Zellij-style floating pane overlay system","description":"# Port Zellij-Style Floating Pane Overlay System

## Why
WezTerm only supports split panes (horizontal/vertical in a tree). Zellij has floating panes that overlay the terminal without affecting the split layout. This is incredibly useful for: temporary command palettes, quick previews, ephemeral agent output, status overlays.

## What to build

### FloatingPane concept
A pane that:
- Exists outside the split tree (not a leaf in the layout)
- Has absolute position (x, y, width, height) relative to the tab area
- Renders on top of split panes (z-ordering)
- Can be moved, resized, minimized, maximized
- Focus: floating panes can receive focus without changing split layout
- Transparency: background can be semi-transparent (terminal cell overlay)

### Data model changes
```rust
// In frankenterm/mux/src/tab.rs
pub struct Tab {
    // Existing: split tree
    pane_tree: Tree,
    
    // NEW: floating panes
    floating_panes: Vec<FloatingPane>,
    floating_focus: Option<usize>,  // Index into floating_panes
}

pub struct FloatingPane {
    pub pane: Arc<dyn Pane>,
    pub rect: Rect,           // Position and size
    pub z_order: u32,         // Higher = on top
    pub visible: bool,
    pub pinned: bool,         // Stays visible when focus changes
    pub opacity: f32,         // 0.0-1.0
}

pub struct Rect {
    pub x: u16,
    pub y: u16,
    pub width: u16,
    pub height: u16,
}
```

### Rendering
Floating panes are rendered AFTER split panes. The render pipeline becomes:
1. Render split tree (existing)
2. For each floating pane (sorted by z_order):
   a. Save affected region
   b. Render floating pane cells over the region
   c. Draw border/shadow

### Keyboard shortcuts (default)
- Ctrl+Shift+F: Toggle floating pane mode
- Ctrl+Shift+N: New floating pane
- Ctrl+Shift+W: Close floating pane
- Ctrl+Shift+Arrow: Move floating pane
- Ctrl+Shift+Alt+Arrow: Resize floating pane

### Wire protocol (PDU additions)
New PDUs for mux server communication:
- CreateFloatingPane { tab_id, rect, pane_id }
- MoveFloatingPane { pane_id, new_rect }
- SetFloatingPaneZ { pane_id, z_order }
- ToggleFloatingPane { pane_id }

## Files affected
- frankenterm/mux/src/tab.rs (floating pane management)
- frankenterm/mux/src/pane.rs (FloatingPane wrapper)
- frankenterm/codec/src/lib.rs (new PDU types)
- frankenterm/wezterm-surface/src/ (render pipeline changes)

## Testing
- Create and position a floating pane
- Verify z-ordering (higher z renders on top)
- Verify focus switching between floating and split panes
- Verify resize constraints (min 5x3 cells)
- Verify PDU serialization round-trip
- Visual test: floating pane renders correctly over splits

## Test framework requirements

### proptest — overlay z-ordering invariants
Use proptest to generate arbitrary sequences of floating pane create/move/reorder/delete operations and verify:
- Z-ordering is always a total order (no two panes share the same z_order after normalization)
- After any operation sequence, the top-most pane at a given (x, y) coordinate is deterministic
- Focus pane is always rendered last (highest effective z-order)
- Removing a pane never changes the relative order of remaining panes

### criterion benchmarks — overlay hit-testing
Benchmark with criterion:
- Hit-test lookup for a point against N floating panes (target: <500ns for 20 panes)
- Z-order sort for N floating panes (target: <1us for 50 panes)
- Full render-order computation for a tab with M splits + N floating panes

### LabRuntime DPOR — concurrent overlay operations
Use LabRuntime with DPOR exploration to verify:
- Concurrent create/delete of floating panes never produces dangling references
- Concurrent move + render never reads a partially-updated Rect
- Focus transitions are linearizable when multiple sources (keyboard, mux protocol) race

## Cross-references
- **wa-1xk9j** — Zellij layout engine analysis: architecture study that informs the floating pane design, including Zellij's SizedFloatingPane model and z-ordering strategy
- **wa-2dd4s.5** — FrankenMux integration: this bead's floating pane system is assembled into the unified FrankenTab model","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:38:54.84411Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:37:47.245742Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s.2","depends_on_id":"wa-2dd4s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:38:54.84411Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s.2","depends_on_id":"wa-2dd4s.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:16.891245Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2dd4s.3","title":"Port Zellij-style swap layouts with stacked panes","description":"# Port Zellij-Style Swap Layouts

## Why
Zellij's swap layouts let users switch between pre-defined pane arrangements with a single keypress. This is transformative for agent swarms: switch from "4-pane grid" to "1 big + 3 small" to "all stacked" instantly.

## What to build

### Layout definitions
Pre-defined layouts in frankenterm.toml:
```toml
[[layouts]]
name = "grid-4"
description = "2x2 grid"
arrangement = [
    { split = "horizontal", ratio = 0.5, children = [
        { split = "vertical", ratio = 0.5 },
        { split = "vertical", ratio = 0.5 },
    ]}
]

[[layouts]]
name = "main-side"
description = "Main pane + side panel"
arrangement = [
    { split = "vertical", ratio = 0.7, children = [
        { pane = "main" },
        { split = "horizontal", ratio = 0.5 },
    ]}
]

[[layouts]]
name = "stacked"
description = "All panes stacked (vertical tabs)"
arrangement = [
    { stack = true }
]
```

### Swap mechanism
When the user presses the swap key (Ctrl+Shift+Space):
1. Current pane arrangement is captured
2. Next layout in the cycle is selected
3. Panes are redistributed into the new layout
4. Focus is preserved on the same pane

### Pane redistribution algorithm
When swapping from N panes in layout A to layout B:
- If B has enough slots: map panes 1:1 (preserve order)
- If B has fewer slots: stack overflow panes in the last slot
- If B has more slots: leave extra slots empty
- Special "main" slot: always gets the focused pane

### Stacked panes (vertical tabs within a split)
Like Zellij, allow panes to be stacked within a split position:
- Multiple panes share one position
- Tab bar at top shows stacked panes
- Only one visible at a time
- Ctrl+Tab cycles through stack

## Files affected
- frankenterm/mux/src/layout.rs (NEW — layout definitions)
- frankenterm/mux/src/tab.rs (swap logic)
- frankenterm/config/src/ (layout config parsing)

## Testing
- Define 3 layouts, swap between them
- Verify pane content is preserved during swap
- Verify focus is preserved
- Test pane overflow (more panes than layout slots)
- Test stacked pane cycling
- Benchmark: swap should be <10ms

## Test framework requirements

### proptest — layout swap invariants (no pane loss during swap)
Use proptest to generate arbitrary sequences of layout swaps and verify:
- No pane is lost during any swap: the set of PaneIds before and after a swap is identical
- Focus is always on a valid pane after swap (never None, never a closed pane)
- Swapping A->B->A restores the original layout structure (round-trip idempotence)
- Pane ordering within stacks is preserved across swaps
- Overflow panes are always reachable (stacked in the last slot, not dropped)

### criterion benchmarks
Benchmark with criterion:
- Layout swap time for N panes across M layout definitions (target: <5ms for 20 panes)
- Pane redistribution algorithm for worst-case (N panes into 1-slot layout)
- Stack cycling latency (target: <100us)

## Cross-references
- **wa-1xk9j** — Zellij layout engine analysis: architecture study that informs the swap layout design, including Zellij's LayoutTemplate and pane redistribution strategy
- **wa-2dd4s.5** — FrankenMux integration: swap layouts are assembled into the unified FrankenTab model with layout cycle support","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:38:59.280409Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:38:05.510179Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s.3","depends_on_id":"wa-2dd4s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:38:59.280409Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s.3","depends_on_id":"wa-2dd4s.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:17.001982Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2dd4s.4","title":"Improved constraint-based resize handling","description":"# Improved Constraint-Based Resize Handling

## Why
WezTerm uses proportional resizing: when a split is resized, children resize proportionally. This doesn't respect minimum sizes and can make panes unusably small. Zellij uses constraint-based resizing which is better.

## What to build

### Constraint system
Each pane has constraints:
```rust
pub struct PaneConstraints {
    pub min_width: u16,   // Default: 5 columns
    pub min_height: u16,  // Default: 3 rows
    pub max_width: Option<u16>,
    pub max_height: Option<u16>,
    pub preferred_width: Option<u16>,
    pub preferred_height: Option<u16>,
    pub fixed: bool,      // Don't resize this pane
}
```

### Constraint solver
When terminal is resized:
1. Compute total available space
2. Assign minimum sizes to all panes
3. Distribute remaining space proportionally (respecting max constraints)
4. If not enough space for minimums: collapse least-priority panes
5. Use topological sort on the split tree for constraint propagation

### Priority-based collapse
When terminal is too small for all panes:
```rust
pub enum CollapsePriority {
    Never,      // This pane never collapses (e.g., main editor)
    Low,        // Collapse first (e.g., status bar)
    Normal,     // Default
    High,       // Collapse last
}
```
Collapsed panes become stacked (hidden but accessible via tab).

### Resize edge detection
When user drags a split border:
- Determine which panes are affected
- Apply constraint solver to just that subtree
- Snap to character boundaries
- Preview resize before applying (render ghost borders)

## Files affected
- frankenterm/mux/src/tab.rs (resize logic — currently proportional)
- frankenterm/mux/src/pane.rs (add constraints to pane)
- frankenterm/bintree/ (tree operations may need constraint awareness)

## Testing
- Resize terminal to various sizes, verify no pane below minimum
- Test constraint propagation through split tree
- Test priority-based collapse (correct pane collapses first)
- Test fixed panes don't resize
- Benchmark: resize calculation should be <1ms
- Property test: sum of pane widths always equals terminal width

## Test framework requirements

### proptest — resize constraint satisfaction
Use proptest to generate arbitrary split trees with random constraints and terminal sizes, then verify:
- Minimum sizes are always respected: no pane is smaller than its min_width/min_height
- Total area is preserved: sum of all pane widths in a horizontal split equals parent width (and analogously for vertical splits)
- Fixed panes never change size after any resize operation
- Constraint solver is deterministic: same inputs always produce same layout
- Collapse priority is respected: higher-priority panes collapse only after all lower-priority panes have collapsed

### criterion benchmarks — resize computation
Benchmark with criterion:
- Constraint solver execution time for N-pane split tree (target: <500us for 50 panes)
- Resize edge detection for a split tree with M borders (target: <100us)
- Full resize cycle: terminal resize event to layout committed (target: <1ms)

## Cross-references
- **wa-1xk9j** — Zellij layout engine analysis: architecture study that informs the constraint-based resize design, including Zellij's constraint propagation algorithm
- **wa-2dd4s.5** — FrankenMux integration: the constraint system is integrated into the unified FrankenTab model","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:03.391484Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:38:22.971727Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s.4","depends_on_id":"wa-2dd4s","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:03.391484Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2dd4s.4","depends_on_id":"wa-2dd4s.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T07:39:17.136107Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}

@@ -218,9 +218,9 @@ The diagnostic schema separates three failure domains:
 
 | Domain | Log Target | Example |
 |--------|-----------|---------|
-| Session lifecycle | `wa::handoff handoff.error.*` | suspend/resume phase violation |
-| Command execution | `wa::handoff handoff.cmd.*` | spawn failure, non-zero exit |
-| Terminal state | `wa::session` (terminal_session.rs) | raw mode, alt screen errors |
+| Session lifecycle | `ft::handoff handoff.error.*` | suspend/resume phase violation |
+| Command execution | `ft::handoff handoff.cmd.*` | spawn failure, non-zero exit |
+| Terminal state | `ft::session` (terminal_session.rs) | raw mode, alt screen errors |
 
 This separation allows fast triage: "was this a TUI lifecycle bug or did the
 subprocess fail?" is answerable from log target alone.
